channel.title: NYT > Top Stories
channel.copyright: Copyright 2026 The New York Times Company

item.title: Trump Says U.S. Will Help Stranded Ships Leave Strait of Hormuz
item.description: President Trump offered few details on how the new program, which he called Project Freedom, would work, but said interference would be dealt with “forcefully.”
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs, David E. Sanger and Neil MacFarquhar
item.category: United States Defense and Military Forces
US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Reuters
item.media:description: Vessels in the Strait of Hormuz, near Musandam, Oman, on Friday.

item.title: Trump Faces the Complicated Reality of a Costly, Unpopular War in Iran
item.description: President Trump’s predictions of a relatively short-term conflict with minimal economic consequences appear to be crumbling.
item.dc:creator: Zolan Kanno-Youngs
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
United States Defense and Military Forces
item.media:credit: Tom Brenner for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump, on Saturday in Florida, is facing the reality that many Americans in a midterm election year disapprove of the war with Iran.

item.title: Oil Prices Jump While Stock Futures Inch Up
item.description: Oil prices fell and futures on the S&P 500 rose slightly on Sunday as investors reacted to continued uncertainty about the war in Iran.
item.dc:creator: The New York Times
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Charles Krupa/Associated Press

item.title: Inquiry Into Fed Chair Jerome Powell Could Be Resurrected, Jeanine Pirro Says
item.description: Jeanine Pirro, the U.S. attorney in Washington, dropped an inquiry into Jerome Powell last month after a federal judge blocked her grand jury subpoenas.
item.dc:creator: Minho Kim
item.media:credit: Anna Rose Layden for The New York Times
item.media:description: Jerome H. Powell, the Federal Reserve chair, has resisted President Trump’s demands that the central bank slash interest rates.

item.title: A Long, Strange Trip: How the G.O.P. Came to Embrace Psychedelic Drugs
item.description: For decades, conservatives were adamantly opposed to the use of drugs like psilocybin and LSD. Now, the Trump administration has made a sharp pivot.
item.dc:creator: Andrew Jacobs
item.media:credit: Pete Marovich for The New York Times
item.media:description: President Trump patted the hand of W. Bryan Hubbard, founder of the advocacy group Americans for Ibogaine, as the podcaster Joe Rogan looked on in an Oval Office ceremony this month.

item.title: Blanche Says Others Who Post ‘86 47’ Message Won’t Be Charged Like Comey
item.description: The acting attorney general said such messages were “posted constantly” and said the Justice Department had gathered other evidence against the former F.B.I. director, but declined to describe it.
item.dc:creator: Devlin Barrett
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard/Reuters
item.media:description: Todd Blanche, the acting attorney general, at the Justice Department last week.

item.title: In a Reversal, Doctors From Countries Under Trump’s Travel Ban Are Allowed to Stay in U.S.
item.description: In a reversal, the Trump administration has exempted foreign physicians from a visa application freeze that was pushing many out of jobs in underserved areas.
item.dc:creator: Miriam Jordan
item.media:credit: Gabriel V. Cárdenas for The New York Times
item.media:description: Ezequiel Veliz, a family doctor from Venezuela, was detained by federal agents when he fell out of legal status because his new visa application had not been processed. He was released 10 days later.

item.title: Rudy Giuliani Hospitalized in Florida in ‘Critical Condition’
item.description: A spokesman for the former mayor of New York City said Mr. Giuliani was critical but stable.
item.dc:creator: Jonah E. Bromwich
item.media:credit: Jefferson Siegel for The New York Times
item.media:description: Rudy Giuliani, the former mayor of New York, is critically ill in a Florida hospital.

item.title: How the Kremlin Lures Africans Into Russia’s War in Ukraine
item.description: A growing number of men across the continent say they are being promised jobs in Russia, only to be forced into the war. Some go as mercenaries, but many more are drawn unwittingly.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg, John Eligon, Zimasa Matiwane and Ed Ram
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times

item.title: Why Are Africans Fighting in Russia’s War in Ukraine?
item.description: Some see a clear explanation: Russia needs more troops, and young men in Africa desperately need jobs.
item.dc:creator: Matthew Mpoke Bigg
item.category: Center for Strategic and International Studies
item.media:credit: Ed Ram for The New York Times
item.media:description: Family members of people recruited by Russia for the war in Ukraine protested in Nairobi, Kenya, in March. The lack of information on missing loved ones spurred their appeal to lawmakers.

item.title: Learning Muay Thai Can Allow Visitors to Stay in Thailand Longer
item.description: Visitors can apply for a five-year visa if they take lessons in “soft power” activities like the Thai martial art. Training does not have to be grueling, and sparring is optional.
item.dc:creator: Nicholas Yong and Lauren DeCicca
item.media:description: Instructors leading a Muay Thai training session at a gym in Bangkok.

item.title: School Cellphone Ban Study Finds Mixed Results
item.description: Cellphone bans got devices out of students’ hands, according to the first large study. But behavior and academics have not improved, at least so far.
item.dc:creator: Dana Goldstein
item.media:credit: Lea Suzuki/San Francisco Chronicle, via Associated Press
item.media:description: Cellphone use declined in schools that adopted strict bans, though test scores did not immediately improve.

item.title: Mariachi Brothers Detained by ICE Invited to Open for Kacey Musgraves After Release
item.description: The teenagers, who had faced deportation after a high-profile detention, took the stage on Sunday thanks to an invitation from one of country music’s biggest stars.
item.dc:creator: Edgar Sandoval
item.media:credit: Tamir Kalifa for The New York Times
item.media:description: Luis Antonio Gámez-Cuéllar wiped a tear from his face after he and his family were released from the Dilley Immigration Processing Center in Dilley, Texas, in March.

item.title: 3 Dead in Hantavirus Outbreak Aboard Cruise Ship, W.H.O. Says
item.description: One case of hantavirus infection has been confirmed in a laboratory, the organization said on Sunday. There are five additional suspected cases.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri and Livia Albeck-Ripka
item.media:credit: Agence France-Presse — Getty Images
item.media:description: The cruise ship MV Hondius off Cape Verde on Sunday. Officials identified the ship as the one with passengers infected by hantavirus.

item.title: What Is Hantavirus, Which Is Linked to the Deaths of 3 People Aboard a Cruise Ship?
item.description: Three people aboard a cruise ship sailing the Atlantic Ocean have died from what health officials suspect were hantavirus infections.
item.dc:creator: Livia Albeck-Ripka, Hank Sanders and Michael Levenson
item.media:credit: BSIP/UIG Via Getty Images)
item.media:description: Hantavirus most often spreads from dried rodent droppings.

item.title: ‘City of Glass’ Is Under Threat From Iran War
item.description: In India, Firozabad has depended on its glassmaking industry for centuries. Fuel prices are starting to shatter businesses already under pressure.
item.dc:creator: Alex Travelli, Suhasini Raj and Atul Loke

item.title: The Widow of the Firefighter Slain by a Would-Be Assassin Speaks Out
item.description: The conspiracy theories around the Butler, Pa., assassination attempt hurt Helen Comperatore, but in this age of distrust, partisanship and social media, she nurtures a conspiracy theory of her own.
item.dc:creator: Kate Zernike
item.media:credit: Maddie McGarvey for The New York Times

item.title: Smog in Phoenix and Salt Lake City? The E.P.A. Is Blaming Asia.
item.description: The Trump administration says the cities shouldn’t be penalized for unhealthy air because pollution can blow in from abroad. Some experts say that’s preposterous.
item.dc:creator: Hiroko Tabuchi
item.category: Far East, South and Southeast Asia and Pacific Areas
item.media:credit: Matt York/Associated Press
item.media:description: Phoenix has long struggled with smog collecting in its bowl-shaped topography. The city failed to meet a federal air pollution standard in 2024.

item.title: Chizi, Standup Comic Exiled in China, Wants to Be More Than Just ‘a Rebel Comedian’
item.description: Having gotten into trouble for making jokes critical of the Chinese government, the standup comic Chizi now lives in self-imposed exile. He’s finding that freedom imposes its own constraints.
item.dc:creator: Li Yuan and Chang W. Lee
item.media:description: Chizi performed for the first time in years in Tokyo in April. The audience went to see a rebel.

item.title: Operation Epic Fury, Meet Operation Colossal Blunder
item.description: With the threat of a future closing of the Strait of Hormuz a drone strike away, the Iranians appear to actually hold the cards.
item.dc:creator: Scott Anderson
item.category: US and Israeli Attack on Iran (2026)
item.media:credit: Nathan Howard for The New York Times

item.title: How Lauren Sánchez Bezos Inspired ‘The Devil Wears Prada 2’
item.description: The real-life Met Gala vs. the one onscreen.
item.dc:creator: Robin Givhan
item.media:credit: Photo illustration by The New York Times

item.title: Tractor-Trailer and Light Post Damaged as Plane Lands at Newark Airport
item.description: The tractor-trailer was on the New Jersey Turnpike on Sunday when it was damaged, the authorities said. Its driver had minor injuries and no one aboard the plane was hurt.
item.dc:creator: Alexandra E. Petri

item.title: 8 Arrested at Protest Over ICE Detention Outside Brooklyn Hospital
item.description: Protesters clashed with the police outside Wyckoff Heights Medical Center, where federal immigration agents brought a detainee for evaluation and later dragged him to a waiting car.
item.dc:creator: Chelsia Rose Marcius and Dana Rubinstein
item.media:credit: Desiree Rios/The New York Times
item.media:description: Protests erupted outside Wyckoff Heights Medical Center late Saturday after federal agents brought a detained man in.

item.title: ‘By the Wind Sailors’ Invade California Beaches by the Thousands
item.description: The brightly colored jellylike marine life, called “Velella velella,” show up every few years when strong winds blow in the right direction, scientists said.
item.dc:creator: Sonia A. Rao
item.media:credit: Aidin Vaziri/San Francisco Chronicle
item.media:description: Velella velella washed up on Baker Beach in San Francisco, Calif., in April.

item.title: E.E.O.C. Investigating Discrimination Claim at The New York Times
item.description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission recently referred a reverse-discrimination complaint by an employee to the agency’s legal unit for review.
item.dc:creator: Rebecca Davis O’Brien
item.media:credit: Lucia Vazquez for The New York Times
item.media:description: The Equal Employment Opportunity Commission is investigating a reverse-discrimination claim filed against The New York Times by a white male employee.
